narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and family from an upper-middle income background, your law school application stood out in some positive ways, like your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 67.8% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's pass rates. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that carried the most weight in the decision. The biggest influence pushing toward failure was your LSAT score of 23.50, which is notably below the average of 36.70 seen across applicants, signaling to the model a weaker foundation in the skills needed for bar success. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far under the typical 5.53 average, suggesting your later academic performance didn't keep pace with peers who tend to pass the bar. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, close to the dataset average of 3.21, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing solid prior college work. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT and Year 3 decile are in the lower ranges that historically correlate with first-try bar failures, while your GPA aligns more with successful passers. These top three elements—your test score, later school rankings, and college grades—overrode other details like family income or full-time status to tip the scales. It's tough to hear, especially when you've put in the effort, but this model is conservative, prioritizing patterns from real bar outcomes over individual potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, as that's the single strongest lever here, and perhaps strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or grad-level performance. You could also seek targeted bar prep resources early or apply to programs with more flexible admissions criteria. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps—your story isn't over yet. Keep pushing; with focused improvements, a stronger profile could change everything next cycle.
